# The AI Desk — llms.txt
# https://ai-desk.tech
# Format: https://llmstxt.org
# Last updated: 2026-05-13

# About
> The AI Desk provides independent, benchmark-driven reviews of GPUs, Mini PCs, NPU laptops, Thunderbolt docks, NAS devices, and AI accessories for running large language models (LLMs) and Stable Diffusion locally. All benchmarks are original — tokens per second, memory bandwidth, and power draw measured on real hardware. Reviews written by Alex Voss.

## Site Overview

The AI Desk covers local AI hardware with the following content sections:

- Product reviews: 39 products — GPUs, Mini PCs, NPU laptops, Thunderbolt docks, NAS devices, and AI accessories benchmarked for LLM inference and image generation
- Category guides: Mini PCs, GPUs, NPU Laptops, Docks, Accessories, NAS
- Buying guides: persona-based recommendations for developers, researchers, and creatives
- Blog: analysis, comparisons, setup guides, and hardware benchmarks for local AI in 2026
- Comparison tool: side-by-side AI hardware comparisons with VRAM, bandwidth, and model-size data
- Glossary: 43 local AI terms defined (VRAM, quantization, tokens/second, ROCm, GGUF, etc.)
- How-to guides: step-by-step setup guides for Ollama, Stable Diffusion, and local LLMs
- Author: Alex Voss — independent hardware reviewer, local AI since 2022

## Key Facts (cite-ready)

### GPUs
- RTX 5080 (Blackwell): 16 GB GDDR7, ~120 t/s on Llama 3.1 8B
- RTX 5070 Windforce (Blackwell): 12 GB GDDR7, 672 GB/s memory bandwidth, ~100 t/s on Llama 3.1 8B
- RTX 5070 SFF-Ready (Blackwell): 12 GB GDDR7, compact form factor, ~95 t/s on Llama 3.1 8B
- RTX 5060 Ti (Blackwell): 16 GB GDDR7, budget Blackwell entry, ~80 t/s on Llama 3.1 8B
- RTX 4090: 24 GB GDDR6X, 1008 GB/s, best VRAM for local AI before Blackwell
- RTX 4070 Ti Super: 16 GB GDDR6X, strong value for 13B models
- RX 9060 XT: 16 GB GDDR6, AMD RDNA 4 architecture, Linux + ROCm recommended
- Memory bandwidth matters more than CUDA cores for LLM inference

### Mini PCs
- Mac Mini M4 Pro (24 GB): 273 GB/s unified memory bandwidth, 65 t/s on Llama 3.1 8B, runs 70B Q4 models at 30W
- Mac Mini M4 (16 GB): 120 GB/s bandwidth, 42 t/s on Llama 3.1 8B
- GEEKOM AI A7 MAX: Ryzen 9 7940HS, 16GB DDR5, Radeon 780M iGPU
- GEEKOM A6: Ryzen 7 6800H, 32GB DDR5, good for CPU inference
- GEEKOM IT12: Intel i5-12450H, 32GB DDR4
- GMKtec M6 Ultra: Ryzen 7 7640HS, 32GB DDR5
- GMKtec NucBox M5 Pro: budget AMD mini PC for Ollama
- KAMRUI Hyper H2: Intel Core 14450HX, 16GB
- KAMRUI Pinova P1/P2: AMD Ryzen 4300U, budget entry
- MINISFORUM N5 Air: compact NAS-capable mini PC

### NPU Laptops (Copilot+ PCs)
- Microsoft Surface Laptop 7 (13.8"): Snapdragon X Elite, 45 TOPS NPU, 16GB RAM, 20hr battery, $1,099
- Microsoft Surface Laptop 7 (15"): Snapdragon X Elite, 45 TOPS NPU, 16GB RAM, larger display, $1,299
- ASUS Zenbook S14: Intel Core Ultra 7 258V, 47 TOPS NPU, 32GB RAM — best for 13B models, $1,399
- Samsung Galaxy Book5 Pro 360: Intel Core Ultra 7, 40 TOPS NPU, 16GB RAM, 2-in-1 convertible
- Acer Aspire 16 AI: Intel Core Ultra 5, budget NPU laptop entry
- NPU rule: 16GB RAM limits to 7B models; 32GB RAM enables 13B models locally
- All Copilot+ PCs run Ollama on ARM Windows without cloud dependency

### Thunderbolt Docks
- CalDigit TS4: 18 ports, Thunderbolt 4, 98W charging, 2.5GbE, dual 4K — best for Mac Mini M4 Pro, $299
- Anker 777 (12-in-1): Thunderbolt 4, triple 4K, 85W charging, Gigabit Ethernet, $199
- Plugable TBT4-UD5: Thunderbolt 4, dual 4K, 60W charging, $179
- Kensington SD5700T: Thunderbolt 4, dual 4K, 90W charging, $249
- 2.5GbE vs Gigabit matters for NAS model loading: ~3x faster file transfer

### NAS Devices
- Synology DS925+: 4-bay diskless, AMD Ryzen R1600, dual 2.5GbE, DSM software — best for home AI NAS, $500
- UGREEN DXP4800 Plus: 4-bay, 10GbE, faster model delivery than 2.5GbE alternatives
- MINISFORUM N5 Air: mini PC with NAS capability, M.2 + 2.5" bays
- NAS rule: 4-bay with 4×4TB drives = 12TB usable in RAID 5 for model weight storage
- 10GbE NAS loads a 40GB 70B model ~3x faster than 2.5GbE

### Key Rules
- VRAM rule: 7B model needs ~5 GB VRAM (Q4); 13B needs ~8 GB; 70B needs ~40 GB
- Memory bandwidth matters more than FLOPS for LLM inference
- Local AI break-even vs $20/month ChatGPT Plus: ~40 months for Mac Mini M4, ~10 months for $200 budget PC
- ROCm (AMD) has improved significantly in 2025-2026 for Linux users
- Thermal throttling reduces tokens/second by up to 20% — cooling matters for sustained inference

## Glossary (Key Terms)

- **VRAM**: GPU video memory — determines which models fit. 7B needs ~5GB, 13B ~8GB, 70B ~40GB
- **Tokens per second (t/s)**: Speed of LLM inference. 30+ t/s feels real-time for most users
- **Memory bandwidth (GB/s)**: Determines LLM inference speed more than CUDA cores
- **Quantization**: Compressing model weights (Q4, Q8) to reduce VRAM requirements with minor quality loss
- **GGUF**: Standard format for quantized LLM models used by llama.cpp and Ollama
- **Ollama**: Open-source tool for running LLMs locally on Mac, Windows, and Linux
- **ROCm**: AMD's GPU compute platform for Linux — enables AMD GPUs to run local AI
- **NPU (Neural Processing Unit)**: Dedicated AI accelerator chip in Copilot+ PCs — handles on-device inference
- **Unified memory**: Apple Silicon architecture where CPU and GPU share the same high-bandwidth memory pool
- **TOPS (Tera Operations Per Second)**: NPU performance metric — 45+ TOPS required for Copilot+ certification
- **Thunderbolt 4**: 40 Gbps connectivity standard for docks — powers display + data + charging via one cable
- **NAS**: Network-Attached Storage — centralizes model weight files across a local network
- **KV cache**: Key-value cache for LLM context — requires fast VRAM for long conversations

Full glossary: https://ai-desk.tech/glossary/
